serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of udon noodles is approximately 200 g of cooked noodles, as commonly served in a bowl at a Japanese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Udon noodles, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20110)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked udon noodles used as primary nutrient reference. Values are per 100 g cooked weight.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Udon noodles (plain, boiled) GI = 55 (low) is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 27.4 × 2.0) / 100 = 30.1.

hypertension.guidance: Cooked udon noodles contain approximately 218 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid high-sodium broths or sauces commonly served with udon.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Udon noodles have a GI of 55 (low boundary) and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~30); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables, and prefer cold or al-dente preparations which lower the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain udon noodles are low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and contain no cholesterol, making them a heart-friendly carbohydrate base when served without high-fat or high-sodium accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Udon (うどん) are thick Japanese wheat noodles made from wheat flour, water, and salt. They are one of the most popular noodle types in Japanese cuisine, characterised by their soft, chewy, and slightly slippery texture. Udon are typically served in a light dashi-based broth (kake udon), stir-fried with vegetables and protein (yaki udon), or chilled with a dipping sauce (zaru udon). In Singapore, udon dishes are widely available at Japanese restaurant chains and casual dining outlets, often served as a main course at around SGD 5–15 per bowl. Nutritionally, cooked udon noodles provide approximately 132 kcal per 100 g, with 27.4 g carbohydrate, 3.5 g protein, and 0.5 g fat. The glycemic index is 55 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is high (~30), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content (~218 mg/100 g) is moderate and increases substantially when served in salty broths. Udon are suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets and are naturally dairy-free, but contain gluten and are not suitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
